title: Listening - Part 3: The Return to Craft
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a master carpenter, Elias Thorne, and a retail analyst, Hannah Vane, about the growing popularity of handmade goods. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview discussing why consumers are moving away from mass production toward unique, artisanal items.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We’re seeing a significant shift back to traditional crafts. Elias, as a carpenter, why do you think people are suddenly willing to wait six months for a handmade table?

Elias: [15]I think we’ve reached a 'saturation point' with flat-pack furniture.[/15] People are tired of items that have no soul and end up in a landfill after three years. When you buy a piece I’ve spent weeks on, you’re buying a story, a connection to the material, and a legacy that your grandchildren might actually inherit.

Interviewer: Hannah, you track consumer trends. Is this just a niche fad for the wealthy?

Hannah: It started that way, but it’s broadening. [16]The real driver isn't just luxury; it’s a reaction against the 'planned obsolescence' of modern manufacturing.[/16] Younger generations, in particular, are becoming more conscious of the environmental cost of 'fast homeware'. They’d rather save up for one meaningful object than fill a house with temporary ones.

Interviewer: Elias, the life of a craftsman isn't easy. Did you ever consider a more stable career?

Elias: Many times! [17]I actually started out in high-end industrial design, but I felt completely disconnected from the end product.[/17] I was just moving pixels on a screen. I needed to feel the grain of the wood under my hands. It was a gamble to go solo, but the psychological reward of seeing a physical object take shape is something an office job could never provide.

Interviewer: Hannah, how are big retailers reacting to this?

Hannah: They’re trying to mimic it, which is the ultimate compliment. [18]You see mass-market brands using 'artisanal' language and intentionally adding 'imperfections' to machine-made goods to make them look authentic.[/18] It’s a bit cynical, but it proves that the consumer's desire for the 'unique' is now a powerful market force.

Interviewer: Elias, do you worry that technology will eventually automate your craft too?

Elias: There are machines that can carve wood with incredible precision now. But [19]what a machine can't do is look at a specific piece of oak and decide how to work around a knot to emphasize its beauty.[/19] That dialogue with the material is purely human. I don't fear the machines; I use them for the boring parts so I can focus on the artistry.

Interviewer: Finally, what’s the future of craftsmanship?

Hannah: [20]I suspect we will see a 'hybrid' model where local workshops use 3D printing for structure but hand-finish everything for soul.[/20] It’s about merging the efficiency of the future with the integrity of the past. Elias and I might disagree on the tools, but we agree that the human touch is what people are ultimately hungry for.

questions.question: Why does Elias believe people are turning away from mass-produced furniture?
options: A) They can no longer afford flat-pack items.
B) They are bored of items that lack character and durability.
C) They want to support local landfills.
D) They find it too difficult to assemble furniture themselves.
questions.explanation: Elias mentions people are tired of items with 'no soul' that end up in landfills quickly.

questions.question: According to Hannah, the main reason for the rise in handmade goods is
options: A) a desire for status among the very wealthy.
B) a lack of availability in traditional retail stores.
C) a protest against products designed to break easily.
D) a misunderstanding of environmental costs.
questions.explanation: She calls it a reaction against 'planned obsolescence'—the manufacturing of goods with a short lifespan.

questions.question: What led Elias to leave his career in industrial design?
options: A) a lack of financial stability in the industry
B) a feeling of detachment from the items he created
C) his inability to master modern design software
D) a desire to work in a larger corporate office
questions.explanation: He says he felt 'completely disconnected from the end product' while moving pixels on a screen.

questions.question: What is Hannah's view on big retailers using 'artisanal' language?
options: A) It is a sign that they genuinely value craftsmanship.
B) It is a calculated attempt to exploit a trend.
C) It is a necessary step to help small workshops.
D) It is confusing for the average consumer.
questions.explanation: She describes the brands adding 'imperfections' to machine-made goods as 'a bit cynical'.

questions.question: Elias believes that machines will never replace humans because they
options: A) lack the precision required for fine carpentry.
B) cannot be used for the more repetitive tasks.
C) are unable to make artistic judgments about wood.
D) are too expensive for small workshops to maintain.
questions.explanation: He argues a machine can't decide how to work around a knot in the wood to emphasize its beauty.

questions.question: Hannah predicts that the future of the industry will involve
options: A) the complete removal of hand-finishing techniques.
B) a total reliance on 18th-century tools.
C) a combination of modern technology and human skill.
D) a decline in the public hunger for the 'human touch'.
questions.explanation: She suggests a 'hybrid' model using 3D printing for structure but finishing by hand.
